food_name: Tahu Tempe Bacem (Javanese Sweet-Braised Tofu and Tempeh)
aliases: Indonesian sweet braised tofu and tempeh

english: Indonesian sweet braised tofu and tempeh

food_subgroup: Sweet-braised and shallow-fried tofu and tempeh; plant-based protein dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Tahu Tempe Bacem. Closest parent would be a braised soy-based dish; no exact or near-exact FoodOn entry identified in the current ontology.
ontology.foodex2_description: Soybean products — closest parent used for Javanese sweet-braised tofu and tempeh (tahu tempe bacem); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this composite dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GCI (soybean products) used as closest parent. No dedicated code for tahu tempe bacem exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Javanese composite dish.

preparation_method: simmered in palm sugar and kecap manis broth

serving.description: 1 serving = ~150g (2 pieces firm tofu ~75g + 2 pieces tempeh ~75g, after braising and brief shallow-frying)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Tahu Tempe Bacem is approximately 150g, comprising two pieces of braised firm tofu (~75g) and two pieces of braised tempeh (~75g), typically served as a protein side dish alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (SEA) — tofu and tempeh component data; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredient cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: FAO / INFOODS; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for firm tofu and tempeh, combined with component calculation for palm sugar, kecap manis, and coconut milk braising liquid. Per-100g values represent the composite dish after braising and shallow-frying. Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for tofu (FDC ID 172443) and tempeh (FDC ID 174272).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimates for soy-based foods and palm sugar — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu-based dishes and palm sugar (GI ~35); peer-reviewed literature on soy protein and glycaemic response
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tahu Tempe Bacem in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~50 is estimated based on: (1) palm sugar GI ~35 (University of Sydney GI Database, Coconut/palm sugar entry); (2) soy protein and fat content moderating glycaemic response; (3) tempeh fermentation reducing glycaemic impact. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 24) / 100 = 12.0, rounded to 11.0 per input source. Confidence: 0.9 (medium-high analogous estimate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~333mg per 100g (~500mg per 150g serving) primarily from kecap manis (sweet soy sauce) and braising liquid salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Tahu Tempe Bacem to one serving (~150g) and request reduced kecap manis in preparation, as the ~500mg sodium per serving represents approximately 22% of the 2,300mg daily sodium limit recommended by clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~50 (low-moderate), GL ~11 per serving; palm sugar GI ~35; high plant protein (14g per serving) and soy fat moderate postprandial glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tahu Tempe Bacem is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as a protein side dish alongside a moderate portion of rice; the low-GI palm sugar (~35), high soy protein (14g per serving), and PUFA-dominant fat profile collectively attenuate the glycaemic response, yielding a GL of ~11 per 150g serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: PUFA-dominant fat profile from soy; low saturated fat (1.5g per 100g); zero cholesterol; isoflavone-rich (~56mg per serving); plant-based protein source
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tahu Tempe Bacem is a cardiovascular-favourable protein choice due to its PUFA-dominant fat profile, negligible saturated fat (1.5g/100g), zero cholesterol, and high isoflavone content (~56mg per serving); soy isoflavones (genistein and daidzein) have demonstrated modest LDL-cholesterol-lowering effects in clinical studies, though sodium intake (~500mg per serving) should be monitored in patients with heart failure or fluid retention.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Isoflavone-rich (~56mg per serving from tofu and tempeh); PUFA-dominant fat; fermentation-enhanced bioavailability from tempeh
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tahu Tempe Bacem provides approximately 56mg of soy isoflavones (genistein and daidzein) per 150g serving, which have demonstrated mild anti-inflammatory activity in rheumatoid arthritis clinical studies; the PUFA-dominant fat profile and fermentation-enhanced nutrient bioavailability from tempeh further support its suitability as an RA-appropriate plant protein.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tahu Tempe Bacem is a traditional Javanese sweet-braised tofu and tempeh dish originating from Central Java, Indonesia, particularly associated with the royal culinary traditions of Yogyakarta and Solo. The dish is prepared using the bacem technique: firm tofu and tempeh are simmered together in a spiced broth of palm sugar, kecap manis (sweet soy sauce), coconut milk, coriander, galangal, and bay leaves until the liquid is fully absorbed and the ingredients are deeply caramelised. After braising, the tofu and tempeh are briefly shallow-fried to develop a sticky, dark, caramelised exterior with a tender interior. The flavour profile is distinctively sweet-savoury with earthy, fermented notes from the tempeh and a rich caramel depth from the palm sugar. Per 150g serving (2 pieces tofu + 2 pieces tempeh): approximately 300 kcal, 14g plant protein, 12g fat (PUFA-dominant from soy), 24g carbohydrate, 16g sugar (palm sugar GI ~35), GI ~50, GL ~11, ~500mg sodium. The dish is vegan, halal, and dairy-free. It is isoflavone-rich (~56mg per serving of genistein and daidzein), making it clinically relevant for cardiovascular health and mild anti-inflammatory benefit. Tempeh fermentation enhances nutrient bioavailability. Suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management (low-moderate GI, high plant protein), cardiovascular health (PUFA-dominant, zero cholesterol), and as a general high-quality plant protein source. Available at Indonesian restaurants in Singapore. Source: FAO/INFOODS SEA + USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-validation.

tahu_tempe_bacem: Indonesian sweet braised tofu and tempeh

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for firm tofu and tempeh, supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cross-validation. The dish is a composite preparation; per-100g values represent a calculated estimate for the braised and fried product rather than a direct laboratory measurement of the finished dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for palm sugar and soy-based foods. Confidence set to medium (0.85) consistent with input record.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite braised tahu tempe bacem dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024; the fat profile is described qualitatively as PUFA-dominant based on the soy base ingredients. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content of the finished braised and fried dish is not reported in the available approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy tofu and tempeh entries. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based (vegan) dish containing no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_c_mg and vitamin_a_mcg_rae are 0.0: confirmed negligible in tofu and tempeh per FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy; the braising spices contribute trace amounts below reporting threshold. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: tofu and tempeh contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.06 per 100g: tempeh fermentation by Rhizopus oligosporus may produce trace B12 analogues; value sourced from FAO/INFOODS SEA tempeh entry. 8. GI ~50 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for tahu tempe bacem exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 9. FoodEx2 code A0GCI used as closest parent (soybean products); no dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for this composite dish. 10. FoodOn URI is null; no exact or near-exact FoodOn entry identified for tahu tempe bacem.
